techniques.evidence_quote: they executed two standard reconnaissance commands against the AWS Simple Email Solution (SES) and Amazon Simple Storage Service (S3): GetSendQuota, ListBuckets
GetBucketVersioning, GetBucketReplication, GetBucketObjectLockConfiguration, GetBucketLogging, GetBucketRequestPayment, GetAccelerateConfiguration
the attackers most likely stole solely 2 GB of data, compared to the over 1 TB of data available on the bucket
Your files are downloaded and backed up on our servers
the attacker utilized the command DeleteBucket to erase all buckets, leaving behind the previously mentioned ransom note
Send us 0.2 Bitcoin (BTC) to our Bitcoin addresses... To recover your lost files and avoid leaking it
the attacker left a ransom note

attack_chain.description: On January 18, 2024, the attacker ran GetSendQuota against AWS SES and ListBuckets against S3 to enumerate available resources and understand the attack surface.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: they executed two standard reconnaissance commands against the AWS Simple Email Solution (SES) and Amazon Simple Storage Service (S3): GetSendQuota, ListBuckets

attack_chain.technique_name: Cloud Infrastructure Discovery — Bucket Configuration Enumeration
attack_chain.description: Before deleting buckets on February 5, 2024, the attacker queried detailed bucket configuration APIs to check whether versioning, replication, object lock, logging, and other recovery mechanisms were enabled — confirming that deletion would be unrecoverable.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: GetBucketVersioning (versioning was not enabled), GetBucketReplication, GetBucketObjectLockConfiguration, GetBucketLogging, GetBucketRequestPayment, GetAccelerateConfiguration

attack_chain.description: The attacker downloaded approximately 2 GB of data from the S3 buckets prior to deletion, to be used as leverage in the ransom demand.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attackers most likely stole solely 2 GB of data, compared to the over 1 TB of data available on the bucket

attack_chain.description: On February 5, 2024, the attacker called DeleteBucket to destroy all S3 buckets, permanently eliminating the victim's data and making recovery impossible.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker utilized the command DeleteBucket to erase all buckets, leaving behind the previously mentioned ransom note

attack_chain.technique_name: Data Encrypted for Impact (Ransom Note & Fraudulent Recovery Binary)
attack_chain.description: The attacker deposited a ransom note and a fraudulent 'recovery' binary (recoveryAKIAZ5ESTHWPHSNVAXZ7.tgz) in place of the deleted buckets. The binary is a scam that only reads a local aws.txt file to simulate recovery activity with no actual network calls, designed to coerce the victim into paying 0.2 BTC.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The whole 'recovery' binary is just a neat trick the attackers use to trick the victims into paying the ransom... not a single domain is contacted, nor was a network connection done

initial_access_vector: Unknown — the root cause of initial credential compromise is not described in the source; the attacker possessed valid AWS credentials (IAM access key) at the time of first observed action on January 18, 2024.
persistence_mechanism: None observed in the source text.
privilege_escalation: None observed in the source text.
